mnemonics.title: Digital Signature Properties
mnemonics.content: A-IN: Authentication, Integrity, Non-repudiation - Remember 'A for Authenticity, I for Integrity, N for Non-repudiation'

mnemonics.title: PKI Components
mnemonics.content: CAke RAised To Serve Users: CA (Certificate Authority), RA (Registration Authority), CRL (Certificate Revocation List), VA (Validation Authority), SP (Subscriber), Relying Party

mnemonics.title: Key Management Phases
mnemonics.content: G-D-S-U-R-D: Generate, Distribute, Store, Use, Rotate, Destroy - 'Good Dogs Stay Under Rigid Discipline'

acronyms.usage: Framework for managing digital certificates and public keys
Trusted entity that issues digital certificates
Entity that verifies identity before certificate issuance
List of revoked certificates published by CA
FIPS standard for digital signatures based on discrete logarithm
Digital signature algorithm using elliptic curve cryptography

visualTips.visualization: Draw a flow: Message → Hash Function → Hash → Encrypt with Private Key → Digital Signature → Send. Verification: Extract Signature → Decrypt with Public Key → Compare with fresh Hash of received Message → Match = Valid
Think of it like a government: Root CA (President) → Intermediate CA (State Gov) → End Entity (Citizen). Trust flows downward from trusted Root.
Picture a circle: Generation → Distribution → Active Use → Rotation/Backup → Revocation/Retirement → Destruction → New Generation (cycle continues)
Stack of cards: Each certificate validates the one above it. Bottom card = your certificate, middle = intermediate CA, top = Root CA (trusted anchor)

analogies.analogy: A handwritten signature is like a rubber stamp - it looks the same regardless of what document it's on. A digital signature is like a wax seal on an envelope - if someone even opens the envelope slightly, the wax breaks, showing tampering.

Think of a private key like a personal seal (like in ancient times). Only you have it, and anything you 'seal' with it is proven to come from you. If someone else gets your seal, they can forge your documents.
A Certificate Authority is like a notary public. You bring your ID, they verify it and stamp a document saying 'This public key belongs to this person' - and everyone trusts the notary's judgment.

Key rotation is like changing the locks on your house periodically. Even if someone copied a key years ago, they can't enter after you change the locks. Limits damage from any single key being compromised.
